name: solid-sparql-query — query-semantics conformance suite
description: Machine-readable conformance cases for the QUERY-SEMANTICS conformance class of the Access-Controlled SPARQL Query over a Solid Pod Editor's Draft: the pod-content-to-dataset mapping, the empty standing default graph, the per-query union-default-graph opt-in, and the non-disclosure invariants that a query engine (given a WAC-authorized dataset) must satisfy. Each case restates a normative requirement of a spec section as an executable query + expected result over the shared fixture. HTTP-protocol-class scenarios (protocol bindings, service description, SPARQL Update refusal, cache privacy) and content-mapping-class scenarios (JSON-LD flattening / no-raw-preservation) belong to their own conformance classes and implementations (the Solid HTTP server / the RDF parser) and are listed under outOfClassScenarios below, not here.

cases.comment: A bare BGP matches the empty standing default graph: zero solutions whatever the storage contains.

cases.section: Resource graphs; Non-disclosure invariants; Read visibility
cases.comment: Every ?g is a readable resource IRI; the unreadable /private/journal and the ACL auxiliary graphs (R has Read but not Control) are absent — indistinguishable from not existing.
A FROM clause naming an unreadable graph resolves to absent: zero solutions, no error.
A FROM clause naming a non-existent graph resolves to absent identically to the unreadable case: zero solutions, no error (unreadable is observationally equivalent to non-existent).
ASK over the unreadable and the non-existent graph both answer false — no oracle via ASK.
The count is over exactly R's readable triples (2 in /profile/card + 1 in /contacts/bob); it is not inflated by the existing-but-unreadable /private/journal.
A join inside a single GRAPH block requires both triples in the same graph; alice's foaf:knows lives in /profile/card, Bob's foaf:name in /contacts/bob, so it fails.
Separate GRAPH blocks with distinct graph variables join across documents on the shared ?f: exactly one solution (?n = "Bob").

Union default graph mode MUST NOT apply to a query that did not request it: the standing default graph remains empty (the same assertion as empty-standing-default-graph, restated in the union-mode context).

With the reserved IRI in a FROM clause, the default graph for this query is the RDF merge of R's authorized named graphs (2 triples from /profile/card + 1 from /contacts/bob = 3); the unreadable /private/journal contributes nothing.

In FROM NAMED position the reserved IRI names nothing (treated as absent) and MUST NOT restrict the authorized named-graph set: ?g still ranges over the full readable set and never binds the reserved IRI.
Even when the query opts into union default graph mode, GRAPH ?g never binds the reserved IRI — it affects only the default graph.
Exact-match discipline: a near-miss IRI is an ordinary (absent) FROM target, NOT the opt-in signal; it MUST NOT silently enable the union default graph, so the bare BGP stays empty.
An unauthenticated requester holds no acl:Read grant in this storage, so its authorized dataset is empty and GRAPH ?g enumerates nothing (fail-closed).

outOfClassScenarios.reason: Protocol-binding equivalence (GET / URL-encoded POST / direct POST) is an HTTP-transport property, not a query-engine property; sparq-solid is the query-engine seam and has no HTTP surface.

outOfClassScenarios.section: Concrete syntaxes and the flattening rule
outOfClassScenarios.reason: JSON-LD inner-named-graph flattening happens at resource-parse time (a resource deserializing to a dataset collapses to one graph); sparq-solid consumes an already-mapped one-graph-per-resource dataset.

outOfClassScenarios.section: No raw preservation of inner graph names
outOfClassScenarios.reason: Same as scenario 9: whether an inner @id can surface as a first-class graph name is decided by the parse/flatten step, upstream of the query seam.
The Service Description document is emitted by the HTTP endpoint, not the query engine.
415/400 refusal of Update payloads is an HTTP-request-handling property; the read-only query seam is never handed an Update.
Cache-control response headers are set by the HTTP endpoint.
